sentences.base_language: We arrive at the restaurant and sit at a table.
sentences.target_language: نصل إلى المطعم ونجلس على طاولة.
sentences.literal: We-arrive to the-restaurant and-we-sit on table.
sentences.grammarNote: نَصِل (naṣil, we arrive) — Form I of root و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l, to arrive/connect): the initial و drops in the present tense (called a waaw-initial verb). المَطعَم (al-maṭʿam, the restaurant) — مَفعَل place-noun from ط-ع-م (to eat/feed): place of eating. نَجلِس عَلى (najlis ʿalā, we sit on) — عَلى (on/upon) is the standard preposition with sitting at a surface.

sentences.base_language: The waiter comes and gives us the menu.
sentences.grammarNote: يَأتِي (yaʾtī, he comes) — Form I of root أ-ت-ي (ʾ-t-y, to come): a defective verb ending in ي. النَّادِل (an-nādil, the waiter) — فَاعِل active participle of ن-د-ل (to hand/pass food). يُعطِينَا (yuʿṭīnā, he gives us) — Form IV of ع-ط-و/ي (to give): أَعطَى (to give) + suffix نَا (us). قَائِمَة الطَّعَام (qāʾimat aṭ-ṭaʿām, menu) — iḍāfa: list + food.

sentences.base_language: I would like hummus with bread, please.
sentences.target_language: أريد حمصاً مع خبز، من فضلك.
sentences.romanized: urid hummusan ma'a khubz, min fadlak.
sentences.literal: I-want hummus with bread, from your-favor.
sentences.grammarNote: حمصاً (hummusan) has the tanwin ending ـاً (an) because it is an indefinite object in a formal/standard sentence. This case ending signals it is the direct object and indefinite. In everyday spoken Arabic this ending is usually dropped.

sentences.base_language: And I will have grilled chicken, please.
sentences.target_language: وأنا سآخذ دجاجاً مشوياً، من فضلك.
sentences.romanized: wa-ana sa-akhudh dajajan mashwiyyan, min fadlak.
sentences.literal: And-I will-take chicken grilled, from your-favor.
sentences.grammarNote: دَجَاجاً مَشوِيَّاً (dajājan mashwiyyan, grilled chicken) — both words carry indefinite accusative tanwin ـاً because دَجَاج is an indefinite direct object and مَشوِيّ is its adjective. مَشوِيّ (mashwī, grilled) — passive participle مَفعُول pattern from ش-و-ي (sh-w-y, to grill/roast): شَوَى (he grilled). Adjective follows and agrees with the noun in case, gender, and definiteness.

sentences.base_language: Would you like something to drink?
sentences.grammarNote: تريدون (turidun) is the plural 'you want' — addressing more than one person. Arabic has distinct verb forms for singular (تريد), dual (تريدان), and plural (تريدون). This plural form is used when speaking to a group.

كوبَين (kubayni) is the dual form of كوب (kub, glass/cup). Arabic has a dedicated dual suffix ـَين (-ayn) for exactly two of something, so you never need to say 'two' separately — the word itself carries that meaning.

sentences.base_language: The food arrives and it smells wonderful.
sentences.grammarNote: يَصِل (yaṣil, it arrives) — present tense Form I of و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l): initial و drops in present (a weak verb). رَائِحَتُهُ رَائِعَة (rāʾiḥatuhu rāʾiʿa, its smell is wonderful) — a nominal sentence: رَائِحَتُهُ (its smell, rāʾiḥa + suffix هُ it) is the subject, رَائِعَة the predicate. رَائِعَة (rāʾiʿa, wonderful, feminine) agrees with رَائِحَة (smell, feminine).

الحُمُّص لَذِيذ (al-ḥummuṣ ladhīdh, the hummus is delicious) — a nominal sentence: definite subject (الحمص) + indefinite predicate adjective (لَذِيذ), no verb needed. This is the fundamental Arabic sentence structure for descriptions. جِدَّاً (jiddan, very) — an intensifier; literally 'very much,' from root ج-د-د (to be serious/intense).

sentences.base_language: Waiter, can we have the bill please?
sentences.target_language: يا نادل، هل يمكننا الحصول على الحساب من فضلك؟
sentences.romanized: ya nadil, hal yumkinuna al-husul 'ala al-hisab min fadlak?
sentences.literal: O waiter, is it-possible-for-us the-getting on the-bill please?
sentences.grammarNote: يَا نَادِل (yā nādil, O waiter) — يَا is the vocative particle used to address someone directly. الحُصُول عَلى (al-ḥuṣūl ʿalā, getting/obtaining) — verbal noun Form I of ح-ص-ل (ḥ-ṣ-l, to obtain): حَصَلَ عَلى = to get/obtain. الحِسَاب (al-ḥisāb, the bill/account) — from root ح-س-ب (to count/reckon): حِسَاب (account), حَاسُوب (computer).

sentences.base_language: Of course. The total is fifty dirhams.
sentences.grammarNote: المَجمُوع (al-majmūʿ, the total) — passive participle Form I of ج-م-ع (j-m-ʿ, to gather/add): مَجمُوع = that which has been gathered = the sum. خَمسُون (khamsūn, fifty) — tens in Arabic end in ـُون in the nominative and ـِين in the genitive/accusative. دِرهَماً (dirhaman, dirham — accusative singular) is used after numbers 11 and above in MSA: the noun is singular accusative with tanwin.
